utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright so hey it's nice to see you jack i know you've been experiencing some neck pain could you tell me what happened

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah so i was in a a car accident

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: mm-hmm

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: and i hit in the back and like my my neck went forward and my head went forward really quick and so i've been having some pain ever since

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and so with this pain how long ago was the car accident

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: that was about a week ago

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay with this pain like on a scale of one to ten how severe is it

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i would say it's about a seven

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: it's not a constant pain but like whenever i move my head which is like a lot i i feel it

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright so where exactly is it on the side on the back

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: it's in the back of my neck

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay has anything made it better

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i mean i've tried some ibuprofen that helped a little bit but not too much

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: mm-hmm

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: just about five out of ten

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: mm-hmm alright and did you say whether the pain was getting worse

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i mean it's been staying the same it just gets worse when i'm moving my neck

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay are you able to turn your neck from side to side

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mostly but there is there is pain with it

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and then do you have headaches

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah i had i had a couple early on

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: uh uh

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i have had it lately

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and so does the pain move anywhere like your upper back your shoulder

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no it just stays on monday

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and then any kind of hearing any kind of hearing problems visual disturbances

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay have you do have you ever like played sports before or had like a sports accident before

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah i played football

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: years ago but i i did n't have any issues with my neck though

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and then when you got in the car accident did you end up going to the emergency room

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no i mean i i know that going to the emergency room that like the english rides like five hundred bucks so i did n't want to do that

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: i understand i understand okay was there any like swelling or bruising on your neck

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: not that i know about no other mag of my neck i really ca n't see that

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah okay no just checking okay so if you do n't mind i'm gon na go ahead and do my physical exam so when i press on here on the side of your neck does it hurt

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah a little bit

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay positive pain to palpation of the soft tissues of the neck what about when i press on your back or your shoulders

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no that's fine

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so when you flex your neck when you're touching your chin to your chest does that hurt

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm-hmm

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright positive pain with flexion what about when you move it back

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah that hurts worse

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay okay severe positive pain to extension okay so can you turn your head from side to side does that hurt

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah a little bit

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay positive pain with rotation and then can you touch your ear to your shoulders

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright so positive pain on lateral bending wow this is pretty this is pretty serious not serious necessarily sorry about that so i reviewed the results of your x-ray but the results show no signs of fracture or bony abnormalities but let's go ahead and talk about my assessment and plan for you i believe what you have is something called neck strain for your neck pain i sorry treatments we are gon na go ahead and treat this a bit conservatively your i'm gon na put you on anti-inflammatories motrin six hundred milligrams and you're gon na take that every six to eight hours i also wan na give you a muscle relaxant called flexeril and it's gon na be ten milligrams and you'll take that every twelve hours as needed i'm gon na want you to try your best to to like relax your neck i'm sorry not to strain your neck anymore like to be conservative with how you move about and everything like that i also wan na order an mri just because you said you did n't go to the hospital or anything like that i just wan na make sure that you you're not suffering from like a concussion but this is something that's commonly referred to as like whiplash right your head just like it it just wiped essentially like back and forth to so severely and and that's probably what's causing your pain i think i wan na refer you to either physical rehab or a chiropractor once we get the results of the mri just to make sure that there is n't any impingement of like the nerves or anything like that do you have any questions

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i heard the chiropractors will shake they work

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: i do understand that some people have like reservations about going to the chiropractor but you know we do have some good ones that have like longstanding histories and patients that have had positive results from the experience but if you if you do n't like the idea of that we can consider other options like that rehab and physical therapy

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright any other questions

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright thank you

detail_list.summary: The doctor introduces the patient, Jack Torres, a 40-year-old male presenting with neck pain.
Neck pain
The doctor asks the patient to describe the circumstances of his neck pain.
The patient reports his neck pain began following a motor vehicle accident.
The patient describes the accident as being hit from behind, causing his head and neck to snap forward.
The doctor asks how long ago the accident occurred.
The accident occurred one week ago.
The doctor asks the patient to rate the severity of his pain on a scale of one to ten.
The patient rates his pain as a 7 out of 10.
The patient reports that neck movement exacerbates his pain.
The doctor asks the patient to localize the pain.
The patient localizes the pain to the posterior aspect of his neck.
The doctor asks if anything has improved the pain.
The patient reports that ibuprofen provides some relief.
The patient reports his pain improves to a 5 out of 10 with ibuprofen.
The doctor asks if the pain is worsening.
The patient reports the pain intensity is stable but worsens with neck movement.
The doctor asks if the patient can turn his neck from side to side.
The patient reports pain with neck rotation.
The doctor asks if the patient has headaches.
The patient reports having a couple of headaches early on after the accident.
The patient denies recent headaches.
The doctor asks if the pain radiates to the upper back or shoulders.
The patient denies radiation of pain.
The doctor asks about hearing problems or visual disturbances.
The patient denies hearing problems or visual disturbances.
The doctor asks about history of sports or sports-related accidents.
The patient played football years ago.
The patient denies any neck issues related to playing football.
The doctor asks if the patient went to the emergency room after the accident.
The patient did not go to the emergency room after the accident.
The doctor asks if there was any swelling or bruising on the neck.
The patient denies knowledge of any swelling or bruising.
The doctor initiates the physical examination of the neck.
The doctor asks if there is pain upon palpation of the side of the neck.
The patient reports pain upon palpation of the neck.
The doctor notes positive pain to palpation of the soft tissues of the neck.
The doctor asks if there is pain upon palpation of the back or shoulders.
The patient denies pain upon palpation of the back or shoulders.
The doctor asks if there is pain with neck flexion.
The patient reports pain with neck flexion.
The doctor notes positive pain with flexion.
The doctor asks if there is pain with neck extension.
The patient reports increased pain with neck extension.
The doctor notes severe positive pain with extension.
The doctor asks if there is pain with neck rotation.
The patient reports pain with neck rotation.
The doctor notes positive pain with rotation.
The doctor asks if there is pain with lateral bending.
The patient reports pain with lateral bending.

The doctor notes positive pain on lateral bending, confirms x-rays show no fractures or bony abnormalities, assesses the patient with neck strain, and outlines a plan including Motrin 600mg every 6-8 hours, Flexeril 10mg every 12 hours as needed, conservative movement, an MRI, and a referral to physical rehab or a chiropractor.
Motrin 600 mg every 6 to 8 hours and Flexeril 10 mg every 12 hours as needed.
The doctor asks if the patient has any questions.
The patient expresses concern about chiropractic treatment.
The doctor addresses the patient's concerns about chiropractic care and offers physical therapy as an alternative.
The doctor asks if the patient has any other questions.
